text:
# Copyedit Review: Nobody Reads Code Anymore

**Reviewer:** Fake Matt (copyedit skill)
**Date:** 2026-06-04
**Source:** /Users/mattheweisner/zerg/.worktrees/blog-nobody-reads-code/web/src/public/content/blog/nobody-reads-code-anymore.md
**Anchors:** writing_style.md + AI Writing Cleanup + voice fingerprint

## Summary

- 8 findings total: 3 HIGH, 3 MEDIUM, 2 LOW
- Top issue: Two "This is not X. It is Y" contrast clichés — explicit ban in writing_style.md → Contrast Cliches; the closer line "anti-AI / anti-pretending" is defensible-as-punchline, the middle "trust the agent and skim / make the deterministic checks" instance is not.
- Quickest wins:
  - Trim body image caption from ~30 words to a ≤20-word billboard
  - Convert the "trust the agent and skim" contrast frame to a direct claim
  - Roll filler-word sweep: "just" 2x, "actually" 2x

**External claims note:** External claims (3 items) appear unverified from artifact alone. Verify against source URL before publish — see `feedback_verify_claims_before_review.md` / `feedback_citation_integrity_fabrication.md`. Items: (1) Jake Nations title + Netflix affiliation + talk title "The Infinite Software Crisis" — this is the same Nations-talk-title surface that triggered the v10 review incident, treat with care; (2) Google ArXiv paper at 2504.09691 — the claim it "describes building the pipeline specifically to keep review loads manageable" needs source check; (3) internal "36 routes / 30% first-pass" stat — verify against the engagement record.

## Scorecard

- Total: 77/100
- Cap applied: none

| Dimension | Score | Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| Truth / Scope / Launch Safety | 18/25 | External claims (Nations affiliation+title, Google paper summary, internal 30% stat) all need re-verification before publish. Nations title specifically flagged by prior incident memory. |
| Audience Fit / Positioning | 17/20 | Strong pulp shape for engineering leaders shipping agent-generated code. Zerg tie-in frames the company as investing in the better path (no Zerg-voice trap). Closer "Stop pretending nobody is skimming" circles back to title cleanly. |
| Concrete Usefulness / Conversion | 12/15 | One CTA (reach out) + audit-prompt prescription. Numbers concrete (30% first-pass, 36 routes). CTA verb could be sharper than "reach out." |
| Voice / Copy Quality | 11/15 | Two contrast clichés violate writing_style.md. Filler words light but present. Em-dash density borderline. Some sentences run long in the "review surface" paragraph. |
| Visual / Interaction Quality | 11/15 | Single body image; caption exceeds pulp 20-word billboard target and duplicates body framing. |
| Implementation / Operability | 8/10 | Companion "See also" link wired. Metadata-drift surfaces (.ts excerpt, seo.description, image alt) need to track the same facts the body cites. |

Top score-impacting fixes:
1. Kill the "The model is not X. It is Y" contrast cliché (HIGH, F2)
2. Rewrite the long body-image caption to ≤20 words and dedup body↔caption (HIGH, F3)
3. Verify Nations title/affiliation/talk-title before publish — prior fabrication incident is the same surface (LOW→pre-publish task)

Learning tags: `contrast-cliche`, `caption-billboard`, `nations-citation-watch`, `filler-sweep`

## Findings

### F1 — "This is not anti-AI. It is anti-pretending." is the banned contrast frame
**Section:** End of the "review surface has to do the work" section
**Rule:** writing_style.md → Contrast Cliches ("This is not X. It is Y.")
**Confidence:** MEDIUM
**Quote:** > This is not anti-AI. It is anti-pretending.
**Issue:** The exact shape the style guide bans. It's defensible because "anti-pretending" is a coinage and the line is load-bearing (it crystallizes the thesis), which is the explicit carve-out in the rule. But it's still a flag because the same paragraph already has a longer cliché-shape sentence (see F2), and stacking two of them in adjacent paragraphs is what the rule is trying to prevent.
**Suggested fix:** Keep as the closer if F2 is rewritten (single instance, load-bearing punchline = within carve-out). If F2 stays as-is, rewrite this one to: "The point isn't to slow AI down. It's to stop pretending we still read what it writes." or simply "Stop pretending we still read what it writes." Author call — flag for awareness.

### F2 — "The model is not 'trust the agent and skim.' It is 'make the deterministic checks...'" cliché frame, not load-bearing
**Section:** Paragraph beginning "This is the work the products we build at Zerg keep being shaped by"
**Rule:** writing_style.md → Contrast Cliches
**Confidence:** HIGH
**Quote:** > The model is not "trust the agent and skim." It is "make the deterministic checks do what humans used to do, so humans can spend their attention where the agent is most likely to fail."
**Issue:** Functional "Not X. Y" construction. The contrast isn't surprising (skimming vs. deterministic checks is the post's central distinction, already established by this point), so it doesn't earn the cliché shape. Also drags two scare-quoted clauses through one sentence.
**Suggested fix:** "The model is to make the deterministic checks do what humans used to do, so humans can spend their attention where the agent is most likely to fail." Or split: "Deterministic checks do what humans used to do. Humans go to the places the agent is most likely to be wrong."

### F3 — Body image caption is 30 words and restates the body
**Section:** Image alt-text under "The bugs that get past skim-review have a different shape"
**Rule:** Pulp register → Caption discipline (≤20 words, dedup body↔caption)
**Confidence:** HIGH
**Quote:** > Before/after: when review went from reading every line to skimming the diff, the bug shape that gets through changed — from typos linters catch to plausible-sounding nonsense that walks past them.
**Issue:** Roughly 30 words and the entire idea ("bug shape changed from typos to plausible-sounding nonsense") is the next two paragraphs of the body. Caption is a billboard, not a paragraph; right now it's a paragraph.
**Suggested fix:** "Read every line: typos linters catch. Skim the diff: plausible-sounding nonsense walks past." Or even tighter: "Bug shape changed: typos → plausible-sounding nonsense."

### F4 — Filler-word sweep (rolled)
**Section:** Whole body
**Rule:** Calibrated rule (mandatory filler-word sweep) + writing_style.md → Economy (Cut filler words)
**Confidence:** MEDIUM
**Quote:** > they **just** do something other than what was asked for // This isn't **just** us // how the data is **actually** shaped // whether the ticket **actually** meant
**Issue:** 4 instances across 4 sentences. Each one tests as cuttable without losing meaning. "just" in the punchline paragraph slightly softens a line that should hit hardest.
**Suggested fix:**
  - "they do something other than what was asked for" (drop "just")
  - "This isn't only us" or "We're not alone here" (drop "just")
  - "how the data is shaped" (drop "actually")
  - "whether the ticket meant what the agent decided" (drop "actually")

### F5 — Long compound sentence in the review-surface paragraph
**Section:** Paragraph beginning "The review surface needs richer diffs"
**Rule:** writing_style.md → Clarity (short sentences) + Scott Adams' Rule 2
**Confidence:** MEDIUM
**Quote:** > The review surface needs richer diffs (what tests exercise this change, what production traces hit this path), typed boundaries so a shape mismatch is the compiler's problem not the reviewer's, migrations dry-run against a prod-schema snapshot, and an agent-authored "what I touched and what I didn't" checklist — each one moves a class of bug out of the human's attention budget.
**Issue:** One sentence carrying four list items, two parentheticals, an em-dash, and a summary clause. The structure is right (list → summary) but the punctuation density makes the reader stop and parse.
**Suggested fix:** Break into a sentence + a real list, or split the summary out: "The review surface needs four things. Richer diffs (what tests exercise this change, what production traces hit this path). Typed boundaries, so a shape mismatch is the compiler's problem and not the reviewer's. Migrations dry-run against a prod-schema snapshot. An agent-authored 'what I touched and what I didn't' checklist. Each one moves a class of bug out of the human's attention budget."

### F6 — "reach out" CTA is the soft version of the available verb
**Section:** Closer of "Where this goes next" section
**Rule:** Pulp register → Single CTA in closer (verb-forward, confident-and-specific)
**Confidence:** MEDIUM
**Quote:** > [reach out](https://zergai.com/contact) — happy to walk through it.
**Issue:** "reach out" is fine but neutral; the pulp register prefers verb-forward, confident, specific ("come argue with us" / "tell us what we got wrong" / "ask us to walk you through it"). The current "happy to walk through it" softens further with a participle hedge.
**Suggested fix:** "[ask us to walk you through it](https://zergai.com/contact)." Or "[grab time with us](https://zergai.com/contact) and we'll show you what's in the gate." Keep "reach out" if the audience skews more enterprise-buyer than pulp-reader — this is a register call.

### F7 — Jake Nations title + affiliation + talk title need verify before publish
**Section:** Paragraph 4 ("Jake Nations, a staff engineer at Netflix...")
**Rule:** writing_style.md → Public Zerg/Competitor Claims (named entity, quoted title) + memory `feedback_citation_integrity_fabrication.md` (the Nations talk-title incident, v10 review 2026-06-02)
**Confidence:** LOW
**Quote:** > Jake Nations, a staff engineer at Netflix, opened a 2025 talk — ["The Infinite Software Crisis"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eIoohUmYpGI) — with the same admission
**Issue:** This is the same surface as the prior fabrication incident: a named individual + employer + exact quoted talk title + YouTube link. The talk-title fabrication was the specific failure mode caught in v10 review. The body text paraphrases what he said (not a direct quote, which is good), but the title appears in quotation marks and as the hyperlink anchor, so any drift between artifact and live video is the same failure shape.
**Suggested fix:** Discussion question — see Q1.

### F8 — Google ArXiv paper claim is summary, not quotation; verify before publish
**Section:** Paragraph beginning "This isn't just us"
**Rule:** writing_style.md → Public Zerg/Competitor Claims (load-bearing reference)
**Confidence:** LOW
**Quote:** > [Google's own report on LLM-driven code migration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.09691) describes building the pipeline specifically to keep review loads manageable as most changes shifted to being machine-initiated.
**Issue:** The artifact alone can't verify the paper says this. The link is load-bearing for the "this is industry-wide" framing.
**Suggested fix:** Discussion question — see Q2.

## Strategic disclosure

These are author/CEO calls, not copyedit findings — flagging for awareness only:

1. **"migration dry-run against a prod schema snapshot is the kind of check we're extending the gate to include"** — explicit admission that one of the four "review surface needs" items isn't shipped yet at Zerg. This is honest scoping and lines up with the post's "anti-pretending" thesis. Keep it if there's a follow-up post planned for when the check ships; otherwise consider whether telegraphing a target serves the strategy.

2. **"Typecheck, lint, and test still live in repo CI"** — minor; reads as scoping, not gap.

Author should weigh in on whether these stay as-is.

## Metadata drift

Body cites several load-bearing facts. Confirm the post's `.ts` `excerpt` and `seo.description` (and the LinkedIn share card if there is one) carry the same numbers and names — and that any image alt-text downstream matches:

- "36 routes autonomously, roughly 30% of files passed both functional and visual checks on the first pass"
- "Jake Nations, a staff engineer at Netflix" + "The Infinite Software Crisis" (2025)
- Google ArXiv ref 2504.09691
- "Our PR-review bot runs a simulated code reviewer over agent-authored diffs"

Per `feedback_blog_metadata_drift.md`, don't review the `.ts` directly here — just confirm the author has cross-checked.

## Interview queue (LOW confidence items only)

### Q1 — Has the Jake Nations title + affiliation + talk title been re-verified against the live YouTube video?
**Source finding:** F7
**Why we couldn't decide:** This surface is the same one that fabricated a Nations talk title in the v10 review incident. Copyedit can't verify from the artifact alone, but given the prior incident specifically here, the answer matters.
**What to ask Matt:** Have you (or someone) clicked through to `youtube.com/watch?v=eIoohUmYpGI`, confirmed Nations' on-screen title slate or Netflix affiliation, and confirmed the exact talk title "The Infinite Software Crisis"? If even one of those three drifted, we paraphrase with attribution instead and drop the quoted title.

### Q2 — Does Google ArXiv 2504.09691 actually say what we're claiming it says?
**Source finding:** F8
**Why we couldn't decide:** The paraphrase ("describes building the pipeline specifically to keep review loads manageable as most changes shifted to being machine-initiated") is plausible but could be a model-summary drift rather than the paper's actual framing.
**What to ask Matt:** Has anyone read the relevant section of the paper to confirm "keep review loads manageable" is what Google's report actually frames the pipeline design around — vs. e.g. throughput, correctness, or cost? If not exact, what's the precise framing we should match?
